articles.title: Efficient targeting of human glial progenitor cells in vivo with engineered AAV vectors and glymphatic delivery
articles.summary: Nature Biotechnology, Published online: 08 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41587-026-03185-2 Viral vectors delivered through the brain glymphatics achieve widespread targeting of glial cells.
articles.content:
Abstract

Treatment of neurological diseases that involve oligodendrocytes or astrocytes would benefit from the selective delivery of viral vectors to their common parent, glial progenitor cells (GPCs). Here, we select adeno-associated virus (AAV) capsids with tropism for human GPCs and demonstrate efficient and widespread delivery of the resultant AAVs throughout the mouse brain through the glymphatic system. In vivo screening of a library of capsid-modified, recombination-reported AAVs in chimeric mice engrafted with PDGFRA-driven Cre recombinase-expressing human GPCs identified a set of AAV5-based vectors that preferentially infect human GPCs and/or their astrocyte and oligodendrocyte progeny in vivo, with minimal systemic infection. To maximize the intracerebral distribution of these vectors while minimizing their dosing and extracerebral spread, we paired intracisternal delivery with systemic hypertonicity to increase glymphatic influx. This method bypasses the blood–brain barrier, delivering AAV directly into the brain parenchyma. Glymphatic delivery of our capsid-modified AAV5s enables efficient transgene delivery to human glia throughout the entire adult mouse brain, with minimal off-target transduction.

Main

A broad variety of neurological disorders are characterized by atrophy and involution of the cerebral white matter, with oligodendrocytic loss and demyelination unaccompanied by adaptive or compensatory remyelination. In a number of these conditions, including disorders as varied as progressive multiple sclerosis (PMS), Huntington disease (HD), juvenile onset schizophrenia and the periventricular leukomalacia of cerebral palsy, dysmyelination evolves despite the persistence of a pool of parenchymal glial progenitor cells (GPCs)1,2. These GPCs, which are also typically referred to as oligodendrocyte progenitor cells but may give rise to astrocytes or oligodendrocytes in humans, appear to lose mitotic and differentiation competence in the setting of these disorders. This may occur by virtue of mitotic exhaustion, as in PMS and late aging, or because of cell-intrinsic defects in maturational pathways, as in HD and schizophrenia3,4,5. To target these cells for therapeutic manipulation, vectors able to deliver genetic payloads to these cells, whether specifically so or to neurons as well, and to target the central nervous system (CNS) only, with little or no systemic transduction, are needed.

Adeno-associated virus (AAV) vectors, the most widely used viral vector in clinical trials and contemporary gene therapy, enable stable expression of therapeutic transgenes in cell types of interest. Natural AAV serotypes can infect many different cell types, which can lead to undesired off-target effects and toxicities. To address this issue, a number of strategies have been developed for evolving serotype-specific binding domains to target prospectively defined cell types of interest. In particular, previous studies have developed approaches toward capsid evolution based on Cre recombinase-dependent identification of viruses evolved to specifically target desired cells6. In particular, viral variants with randomly mutagenized VP1 capsid sequences have been screened for their relative infectivity of given cell types7. However, most of these studies have evolved viral variants either in mouse transgenic reporters with phenotype-selective Cre expression in vivo6,7 or in human cells in vitro8; none have pursued viral evolution against human cells in vivo. Yet, the surface epitopes presented by human cells are quite distinct from those presented by mice, and epitopes expressed in vivo may differ substantially from those targeted in vitro. Moreover, most studies focused on the brain have sought to target neurons; only a few have targeted glial cells8,9. An AAV vector that preferentially targets striatal oligodendrocytes was developed in rats that also infects these cells in macaques9,10; however, this vector requires intracerebral administration and its extrastriatal and human phenotypic selectivities remain unclear. To our knowledge, no prior studies have focused on targeting human glia in vivo and no vectors have been reported that efficiently target GPCs, the precursor to both oligodendrocytes and astrocytes, which comprise 3–4% of all brain cells in adult humans and mice alike11,12,13,14,15.

To develop vectors targeting human GPCs (hGPCs) and their progeny in vivo, we adapted the previously described M-CREATE selection strategy6,7, through which Cre recombinase-expressing cells are used to screen recombination-reported viral variants for cell tropism, but we did so through capsid evolution on Cre-expressing hGPCs in vivo. In particular, we screened viral candidates in human glial chimeric mice, which were neonatally engrafted with human embryonic stem-cell-derived GPCs expressing Cre under the control of PDGFRA, which is selectively expressed by hGPCs in the brain. Our viral candidates were engineered to include Lox66/Lox71-flanked sequences, whose Cre-dependent recombination in targeted Cre-expressing host cells allowed the PCR-based identification of those viral variants that most efficiently infected hGPCs (Fig. 1a–c).

The engineering of capsid-evolved vectors addresses only one aspect of the larger challenge of vector delivery to the brain; viral vectors, however efficient and cell type selective, are only as good as their tissue access. AAV delivery to the brain has typically been limited by poor blood–brain barrier (BBB) permeability and broad systemic leakage, requiring high and, hence, toxic doses. The BBB, in particular, poses a formidable obstacle to the efficient transduction of neurons and glia by systemically administered viral vectors, as it hinders viral entry from the systemic circulation. While serotypes such as AAV9-PHP.B and capsid-modified variants have been engineered to traverse the barrier16,17,18, their clinical use has nonetheless been limited by off-target transduction and the attendant toxicity of systemic infection19, as well as by the immunotoxicity associated with high-dose intravenous administration20,21,22,23,24. Thus, despite the development of creative strategies for circumventing the BBB, the targeting efficiencies for desired phenotypes, particularly so for glia, have remained relatively low.

To establish a practicable strategy for circumventing the BBB using cell-type-selective vectors, we developed a method to co-opt the brain’s glymphatic system25 to achieve widespread viral delivery. The glymphatic pathways comprise a perivascular network of channels through which cerebrospinal fluid enters and perfuses the brain to remove metabolic waste25. Past studies have shown that tracers and nanoparticles can be distributed throughout the brain through glymphatic flow26, suggesting its potential as an alternative route for drug, antibody and vector delivery to the brain parenchyma26,27,28. In addition, parallel studies have indicated that fluid influx to the glymphatic pathways can be potentiated through systemic hyperosmolarity26,27 and access to these influx pathways can be gained through infusion into the cisterna magna29,30,31,32, bypassing the BBB. On the basis of these studies, we combined intracisternal injection of our AAVs with systemic hyperosmolarity and, by doing so, achieved broad AAV distribution throughout the brain while avoiding BBB impedance. This delivery platform accordingly enabled the in vivo screening of a capsid library targeting hGPCs in human glial chimeric mice. We then filtered candidate vectors to exclude those with detectable off-target hepatic infection to identify variants that selectively and efficiently transduced hGPCs and their astrocytic and oligodendrocytic progeny. This approach yielded a panel of gliotropic viral vectors, each optimized to preferentially target a specific state along the human macroglial lineage. Combined with glymphatic delivery, these vectors demonstrate high on-target efficiency in vivo with minimal systemic infection.

Results

Human glial chimeric mice with Cre-expressing hGPCs

To establish human glial chimeric mice in which hGPCs could be identified as such following cell-specific Cre-dependent recombination, we first engineered human embryonic stem cells (hES cells) to express Cre recombinase under the control of PDGFRA, which is selectively expressed by GPCs in the adult brain33. To this end, the Cre gene was knocked into the endogenous PDGFRA locus of hES cells (Genea19 line) by homology-directed repair (HDR)-mediated insertion of donor DNA34. After sequence confirmation of the insert and confirmation by array chromosomal genome hybridization (aCGH) that no major structural variants were introduced into the Cre-expressing hES cell line during editing, the cells were then differentiated into hGPCs using our described protocols35. At 140–160 days in vitro, the hGPCs were transplanted (2 × 105 cells per brain, in four divided injections of 5 × 104 cells each) into the forebrains of neonatal Rag1−/− immunodeficient mice; this technique allows the large-scale replacement of mouse by hGPCs, yielding chimeras in which most GPCs and large numbers of their derived astrocytes are human35,36,37, whereas few if any differentiate as neurons (Extended Data Fig. 1a–d). We generated six human glial chimeric mice for this set of experiments that were colonized with hGPCs expressing Cre recombinase under PDGFRA control. Histological assessment of two of the hGPC-engrafted mice at 14 weeks of age confirmed widespread engraftment by human donor cells, and fluorescence in situ hybridization using RNAScope confirmed the expression of Cre recombinase mRNA by these cells (Extended Data Figs. 1e–h).

Hypertonic intracisternal delivery of AAV5 achieves widespread parenchymal access

We next asked whether we might use glymphatic pathways to access perivascular routes on the brain side of the BBB so as to efficiently deliver AAV5 directly to the brain parenchyma. The glymphatic system comprises the network of perivascular channels by which CSF enters and pervades the brain and then exchanges with brain interstitial fluid to clear metabolic and protein wastes from the brain parenchyma25,38. Previous work showed that glymphatic influx pathways may be used for compound delivery, just as its efflux pathways mediate metabolite clearance27. Therefore, we asked whether the glymphatic network might also serve as a route for viral delivery, circumventing the BBB to allow direct parenchymal entry. We first assessed the utility and perivascular flow patterns of viral delivery through injection into the cisterna magna, which permits direct access to the glymphatic system through anterograde CSF flow30. Critically, we paired intracisternal viral injection with both the systemic delivery of hypertonic saline and head-down prone (Trendelenburg) positioning, which together greatly increase glymphatic influx27. We first used FITC-coupled dextran as a tracer by which to validate the efficacy of this delivery approach and observed widespread tracer entry throughout the forebrain (Extended Data Fig. 2).

We next evaluated the utility of this pathway for viral administration. We assessed forebrain infection following the intracisternal hypertonic delivery of both an unmodified AAV5-serotyped AAV2 inverted terminal repeat (ITR)-based vector (AAV2/5 (ref. 39), hereafter referred to as AAV5) and a modified AAV5 into whose VP1 capsid protein we inserted the neurotropic capsid sequence of AAV-retrograde (AAVrg)40, yielding AAV5-retro. Using our injection protocol, we observed widespread parenchymal infection by each of these control viruses. AAV5-retro particularly infected high proportions of hippocampal pyramidal and corticofugal neurons following a single intracisternal injection (Extended Data Fig. 3a–c), yet unmodified AAV5 similarly infected primarily neurons; both control vectors exhibited relatively sparse glial labeling (Extended Data Fig. 3d–f). Together, these experiments indicated that intracisternal administration of AAV5 under hypertonic conditions enables its rapid, brain-wide perivascular spread, circumventing the BBB to allow direct viral entry into the brain with widespread, efficient cellular transduction. They also confirm prior observations that both unmodified AAV5 and modified AAVs bearing the retro capsid insert are primarily neurotropic41.

AAV5 capsids may be evolved to target hGPCs in vivo

Once we established chimeric mice whose incorporated hGPCs expressed Cre recombinase (Fig. 1a–c) and optimized a strategy for delivering AAVs bearing recombination-sensitive sequences, we next injected the human glial chimeric mice intracisternally with a viral library of candidate AAV5 capsids (10 µl at 1 × 1012 viral genome copies (vg) per ml, 1 × 1010 vg per injection). The library was generated by the ligation of a random sequence of 21 nt inserted after Q574 of VP1, a recognition loop on the sialic-acid-receptor-binding pocket, so as to randomly modify the binding specificity of the capsid, as previously described42. We inserted a GCT codon (alanine) between Q574 and S575 of the AAV5 VP1 capsid to introduce an NheI cut site for insertion of the heptamer-encoding sequences of the library; thus, the engineered AAVs carried random Cap sequences mediating their binding specificity. As such, each of these viral candidates expressed a random heptapeptide insert in the VP1-binding domain, thereby permuting its differential adhesion to target cells. We estimated the resultant library to encode 4 × 105 of these capsid variants. Importantly, the resultant AAV Cap sequences were placed upstream to two poly(A) tails, BGH and SV40 poly(A)sequences inserted in opposite directions, both flanked by two different Lox sequences (Lox66 and Lox71); this allowed the viral DNA to be detected by PCR as either unperturbed or as the product of Cre-dependent reorientation using PCR primers within each poly(A) sequence (Fig. 1d). In virally infected PDGFRA-Cre-expressing hGPCs, the recombination of these sites allowed for the specific amplification of those viruses that successfully transduced the human cells, while viruses that infected mouse cells remained unchanged.

As noted, 10 µl of this viral capsid library (1 × 1012 vg per ml) were then injected intracisternally into each of four 16-week-old mice, after inducing systemic hypertonicity with an intraperitoneal infusion of NaCl (1 M or 5.8%; 20 µl g−1 body weight), with each mouse in a prone Trendelenburg position as described above (Fig. 1e). The mice were killed 1 week after viral delivery and their forebrain viral sequences were assessed by high-throughput sequencing following PCR using two sets of primers: one corresponding to the rearranged virion DNA resulting from recombination in hGPCs, where PDGFRA-driven Cre was active at the time of infection, and a second set corresponding to the native viral DNA sequence, which remained unchanged in the infected mouse cells. Importantly, to exclude viral capsids with notable off-target systemic affinities, the liver was also isolated from the infected mice, gDNA was extracted and those capsids that infected the liver were separately assessed by PCR for each mouse (Fig. 1f). This step allowed us to define and exclude those capsid variant sequences that targeted both the liver and the brain, regardless of their relative affinity for hGPCs. We thereby identified those capsids most selective for human brain cells and for hGPCs and their derivatives in particular while manifesting the least off-target systemic hepatic infection (Fig. 1g and Supplementary Table 1). Choosing the top five most human glia-enriched capsids recovered from each mouse allowed us to identify a set of 20 different capsid variants, which we designated CM1–CM20, that efficiently infected GPCs and exhibited differential infectivity of hGPCs relative to mouse (Extended Data Table 1).

A family of capsid-evolved AAV5s selectively infect GPCs and astrocytes

A number of glial ectodomains are coexpressed by astrocytes and their progenitors; accordingly, they may also be recognized by viruses that infect PDGFRA-expressing GPCs. As a result, we found that our hGPC-selective clones manifested some degree of astrocytic targeting as well: some minimal, such as CM1 (Fig. 2a–e), and some profoundly so, such as CM6, which selectively targeted both GPCs and astrocytes efficiently (Fig. 2f,g). Focusing our efforts on these two vectors, after initial screening that identified them as transducing mouse and human glia, we found that, while some neuronal transduction was exhibited by each, both demonstrated marked gliotropism relative to the unmodified AAV5s from which they were derived (Fig. 2i–k). In contrast, in controls injected with unmodified WT AAV5, almost 50% of infected cells were neurons (Extended Data Fig. 4a); thus, both AAV-CM1 and AAV-CM6 exhibited significantly more avidity for glia than did WT AAV5 (n = 3–4 mice per group; P < 0.0001, one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with post hoc Tukey’s t-tests). Neuronal infection by these AAV5-derived vectors was quantitatively weak; in the same brains and regions in which glial infection was scored, <1% of all neurons were infected (Extended Data Fig. 4b). Together, these data serve to highlight both the relative gliotropism and the greater net glial infectivity of our engineered vectors relative to unmodified AAV5.

Capsid-evolved AAVs were filtered to exclude those manifesting systemic infection

The off-target infection of systemic organs has been a notable source of AAV-associated toxicity19,20,21,22,23,24. Therefore, to minimize the possibility of non-CNS infection, we screened our vectors to identify those with the least infection of systemic tissues. To this end, we included PCR screening for viral sequence of the livers of each animal in which viral candidates were assessed, in light of that organ’s frequent off-target infection by AAVs and associated toxicity. The hepatic viral genome concentrations were compared to those of the targeted brains of the same animals to assess the degree of off-target infection by each of our candidate capsid variants. All candidates manifesting appreciable hepatic infection were excluded from further analysis; this step served as a gatekeeper to identify those capsid variants appropriate for further development and in vivo assessment. Two of these vectors in particular, CM1 and CM6, appeared to allow phenotype-restricted delivery of reporter transgenes to brain glia in vivo, with minimal visceral infection. When delivered intracisternally, these capsid variants exhibited little or no detectable infection of the systemic tissues assessed, which included the liver, spleen and kidneys (Extended Data Fig. 5a–d), as well as the dorsal root ganglia (Extended Data Fig. 5e). In addition, the levels of systemic viremia were far lower in mice injected intracisternally than intravenously, suggesting the much greater efficiency of CNS viral distribution using trans-glymphatic delivery, which may have contributed further to the minimization of systemic off-target infection. Interestingly, the plasma level of AAV-CM1 was found to be significantly greater than that of WT AA5 (indicating less systemic uptake) when assessed at the same point after direct intravenous administration of equivalent viral doses (Extended Data Fig. 5f). Similarly, when hepatocytes were exposed to equivalent doses of AAV-CM1 and WT AAV5 in vitro (2.5 × 105 viral particles per ml, corresponding to the viral genome level detected in mouse plasma following intracisternal delivery), significantly less hepatocellular infection was noted by AAV-CM1 than by WT AAV5 (Extended Data Fig. 5g). Together, these data indicate that hGPC-targeted capsid-modified AAV5s, in particular our prototypic vectors CM1 and CM6, exhibit substantially greater glial infectivity than wild-type AAV5, with lesser albeit persistent infectivity of neuronal populations and little if any off-target visceral infection.

Capsids evolved on hGPCs exhibited efficient human glial infectivity in vivo

The data described thus far describe the relative infectivities of our hGPC capsid-evolved vectors on mouse glia in nonchimeric mice; these experiments demonstrate the marked gliotropism of these vectors relative to the wild-type, unmodified AAV5 from which our variants were derived. We next asked whether these vectors could efficiently target hGPCs and their derivatives in vivo and, if so, whether human cells were selectively infected relative to their mouse counterparts. To that end, we compared the glial targeting efficacy of our capsid-modified AAV5-CM1 to that of wild-type (WT) unmodified AAV5 (AAV2/5) and did so in human glial cells in vivo. In particular, we engineered both WT AAV5 and AAV5-CM1 vectors to express distinct fluorescent reporters in a Cre-dependent manner and then directly compared the relative infectivities of these viruses in hGPCs in vivo, in PDGFRA-Cre human glial chimeric mice. For this purpose, the flex-EGFP reporter43 was packaged into AAV-CM1, while the flex-dtTomato reporter was packaged into WT AAV5 (Fig. 3a); the two reporter viruses were prepared in equal concentrations and equal volumes were then mixed together. A total of 10 µl (1 × 1012 vg per ml) of this 1:1 mixture was then injected into the cisterna magna of 12-month-old mice (n = 3), each of which were neonatally chimerized with PDGFRA-Cre-expressing hGPCs. Then, 3 weeks later, the mice were killed and the relative expression of each vector was assessed in the engrafted human cells (Fig. 3b,c). This analysis revealed that AAV-CM1-EGFP transduced a significantly and substantially higher proportion of hGPCs than did AAV5-dtTomato (Fig. 3d), confirming the superior glial avidity of the AAV-CM1 vector for human glia in vivo.

Having established the enhanced tropism of AAV-CM1 for human glia in vivo relative to wild-type AAV, we next assessed the respective phenotypic biases of our most efficient vectors, CM1 and CM6. We again used adult human glial chimeric mice that were perinatally engrafted with hGPCs produced from unmodified hES cells (line WA09) to assess the avidity of these vectors; we did so by injecting the viruses intracisternally at 16 weeks of age with either AAV-CM1 or AAV-CM6 and killing them 3 weeks later to assess both their transduction efficiencies and preferred phenotypic targets. We found that CM1 exhibited significant bias toward hGPC and oligodendroglial lineage infection, while CM6 was especially tropic for astrocytes (Fig. 4a–c,e–g). In the corpus callosum, AAV-CM1 transduced 5.3-fold more human Olig2+-defined glia than did the parental WT AAV5; 34.9% ± 5.7% of all human Olig2+ glia in the scored field were infected by a single injection of AAV-CM1, whereas only 6.5% ± 1.1% of these cells were infected in WT AAV5-injected mice (n = 4 per group, means ± s.e.m.; P = 0.0014, two-way ANOVA with Šidák’s multiple-comparison test; Fig. 4d). In contrast, AAV-CM6 transduced 3.8-fold more Sox9+-defined astroglia than did WT AAV5 (AAV-CM6: 22.2% ± 3.9%, AAV5: 5.9% ± 2.6%; n = 4 per group, means ± s.e.m.; P = 0.002, two-way ANOVA; Fig. 4h). Thus, while neither CM1 nor CM6 was exclusive for its preferred target, their respective very strong biases towards hGPCs and astrocytes were each highly significant.

The phenotypic selectivity of these vectors was notable in that each exhibited greater tropism to human than mouse cells of each phenotype (Fig. 5). To assess the human selectivity of these vectors, over and above their glial tropism, we assessed the relative infection of mouse GPCs and hGPCs in the corpus callosa of chimeric shiverer mice (MBPshi/shi × rag2−/−) that were neonatally engrafted, injected with AAV-CM1 at 16–17 weeks and killed 3 weeks later. In these immunodeficient and myelin-deficient mice, hGPCs competitively dominate the host mouse GPCs37, such that just over half (52.2% ± 6.0%, n = 3) of all Olig2-defined hGPCs and oligodendroglia were of human origin at the time of killing, consistent with our past studies of hGPC colonization of the neonatally engrafted shiverer brain44 (Fig. 5a–d). We found that, of all Olig2-expressing cells in the scored corpus callosa, 87% ± 3.3% of the infected cells were human and 13% ± 3% were mouse (n = 3 mice per group; P < 0.0001, t-test; Fig. 5d). After normalization for the incidence of GPCs from each species in the chimeric white matter, we found that of all human Olig2-expressing cells in the scored corpus callosa, 30.2% ± 1.1% were infected by CM1. In contrast, just 5.0% ± 0.2% of Olig2+ mouse cells were infected (n = 3 mice per group; P < 0.001, t-test; Fig. 5e). As such, when coresident in the same regions of corpus callosum in roughly the same incidence, the hGPCs were roughly sixfold more likely to be infected by AAV-CM1 than were mouse cells. Thus, this hGPC-evolved vector manifested strong selective tropism for human GPCs relative to mouse GPCs.

CM1 exhibited marked infection of mature oligodendrocytes and their progenitors

In light of the efficient infection by AAV5-CM1 of hGPCs and Olig+ oligodendroglial lineage cells broadly defined, we next asked whether this vector could efficiently infect mature oligodendrocytes, an especially important clinical target for which few competent delivery vectors have been described and even then only in rodent models. To this end, we established mice with humanized white matter through neonatal hGPC chimerization of immunodeficient shiverer mice, as noted above; this model permits the robust oligodendrocytic maturation of their engrafted hGPCs35,36. When the mice reached 16–17 weeks of age, by which time they begin to exhibit initial myelination by the hGPC-derived oligodendrocytes, AAV5-CM1 was delivered intracisternally after induction of systemic hypertonicity. When killed 3 weeks later, robust infection of myelinating human oligodendrocytes, as well as parenchymal astrocytes and persistent hGPCs, was noted in the corpus callosum and major forebrain white-matter tracts (Fig. 6). These data indicate the potential for therapeutic transgene delivery by AAV5-CM1 to mature myelinated oligodendroglia and their progenitors.

Discussion

GPCs are an important target for gene therapy; in addition to being functionally important cells in their own right45,46, they can give rise to both oligodendrocytes and astrocytes and, under select circumstances, neurons as well15,47. While AAVs have been evolved to target a number of cell types of the adult CNS, most such efforts have focused on mouse cells; among those studies focused on human cells, most were necessarily conducted in vitro, as few systems appropriate for targeting human brain cells in vivo have been developed. In this work, we report the development of a set of AAVs, evolved from AAV serotype AAV5, that selectively and efficiently infect hGPCs and their derivatives in vivo, with little off-target CNS or systemic infection. To do so, we selected candidate viruses in vivo, introducing libraries of randomly mutagenized, Cre recombinase-reported AAV5s intracisternally into mice that were neonatally chimerized with Cre recombinase-expressing hGPCs. This approach combined the CREATE strategy6 with human glial chimeric brains36,37 as our platform for in vivo viral selection. We achieved robust glial infection in these mice using delivery through the perivascular glymphatic system25,48 to avoid the BBB and, hence, enable efficient parenchymal entry of our engineered viruses using a clinically feasible strategy.

The hGPCs in these chimerized mice were derived from hES cells that were edited to express Cre recombinase under the regulatory control of the PDGFRA promoter, such that only hGPCs, which specifically express PDGFRA33, expressed Cre. We injected the resultant human glial chimeras intracisternally with a library of AAVs, each of which expressed a random heptapeptide insertion into an AAV-binding domain that regulates target cell adhesion. Each harbored a BGH poly(A) sequence followed by an inverted SV40 poly(A) sequence, flanked by distinct Lox sequences (Lox66 and Lox71), which, upon recombination in PDGFRA-Cre-expressing cells, allowed the amplification of those viruses that successfully transduced hGPCs. This approach identified those variants that selectively infected hGPCs and their derivatives in vivo; the exclusion of some of these on the basis of their infection of host liver then allowed the selection of gliotropic viruses that exhibited minimal infection of nonneural or other systemic phenotypes.

The relative lack of systemic infectivity by these vectors, especially in the setting of trans-glymphatic delivery, is especially important. While there is scant literature concerning gliotropic AAVs, a number of neurotropic AAVs that have advanced to trials, including those modified from AAV9 that cross the BBB, have been associated with notable toxicity, largely because of adventitious infection of the liver and dorsal root ganglia, yielding hepatic dysfunction and pain syndromes respectively19. This toxicity may be the product of the high systemic doses need to achieve BBB passage and the large fraction of those doses that adventitiously infect off-target tissue, such as the liver and spleen, with consequent immune activation. Our use of glymphatic delivery to circumvent the BBB allows much lower viral doses, thus mitigating toxicity. While residual viral escape from the CNS might still permit viral access to the systemic circulation and, hence, peripheral tissues, we found that for our selected vectors CM1 and CM6, visceral infection was so minimal as to be scarcely detectable (Extended Data Fig. 5).

Within the brain, some persistent neuronal infection by our glial-evolved capsid variants was not unexpected. While our capsid modifications included the addition of random sequences with selection for those that bound bipotential GPCs, we did not delete or mutagenize the remaining VP1 sequence or modify its other exposed loops, which are together responsible for its neuronal avidity. This added strong glial tropism to these vectors, without necessarily removing neuronal infectivity; our filtration against liver excluded variants cross-reactive with non-CNS tissues but still allowed variants that retained infectivity for CNS neurons. As a large proportion of potential neurological disease targets involve both neuronal and glial pathology, this may prove a desirable feature in many clinical scenarios. However, in some disorders, glial specificity might be preferred or required. In these cases, further work to selectively remove the neuronal sequences on VP1 may be needed to create vectors whose range of infectivity is entirely restricted to glia. Alternatively, cell-type-selective regulatory elements may be added to further specify gene expression to glial phenotypes of interest. In this regard, a number of enhancers have been identified that can direct gene expression to astrocytes and/or oligodendrocytes49, whose pairing with capsids permitting cell-type-selective infection may provide especially robust vectors for safe clinical use.

To provide these vectors to the brain in as widespread and efficient a manner possible, while minimizing dose and systemic spread, we developed a multifaceted approach to enhance their glymphatic delivery. First, we combined AAV administration with systemic hypertonic saline, a strategy previously shown to improve CNS delivery of antibodies by transiently increasing CSF influx along perivascular spaces27,50,51. In addition, we positioned the mice in the Trendelenburg position after injection, leveraging gravity to facilitate rostral distribution of the vector. This method aligns with previous findings32 demonstrating that maintaining rodents in Trendelenburg for 2 h after intrathecal AAV9 infusion greatly increased gene transfer to cortical and basal forebrain regions, with a >15-fold increase in neuronal transduction relative to upright controls. Our combination of intracisternal viral delivery, systemic hyperosmotic modulation and strategic physical positioning substantially enhanced infection throughout the brain parenchyma. Of note, our approach complements earlier studies using AQP4-knockout mice, which exhibit impaired glymphatic clearance, leading to the increased retention of AAV vectors within the brain following intracerebroventricular administration52. Together, these findings underscore the importance of integrating physiological manipulations with delivery strategies so as to optimize CNS-targeted gene therapy.

An increasing number of neurological disorders are being recognized as either being glial in etiology or having contributory glial pathology. These include not only the pediatric enzymatic and storage disorders, but also the adult myelin disorders, such as PMS and age-related white-matter loss, for which hGPCs and their oligodendrocytic progeny are clear potential targets of glial-directed AAVs. In addition, hGPC-targeted vectors may selectively infect neoplastic stem and progenitor cells within malignant glial tumors, as human glioma cells may arise from resident hGPCs and express high levels of the PDGFA receptor53. More broadly, selectively gliotropic vectors may find value in treating neurodegenerative disorders, given the importance of astrocytic and oligodendroglial pathology in diseases as diverse as HD, schizophrenia, Parkinson disease and Alzheimer disease3,54,55,56, as reviewed previously57. For each of these disorders, potential transcriptional targets have been identified, but the means of selectively and safely transducing a high proportion of the relevant glia in any given brain region in vivo, much less throughout the entire brain and CNS, has not previously been available. The vector selection system described here enables the discovery of AAVs targeting human glial phenotypes of interest in vivo. Furthermore, it advances a glymphatic delivery pathway that entails injection into the cisterna magna with concurrent systemic hypertonicity, a minimally invasive procedure that minimizes extracerebral viral spread while efficiently circumventing the BBB. This combined approach may enable the efficient and selective delivery of therapeutic transgenes to brain cells of interest in a clinically safe and effective manner.

Methods

Generation of PDGFRA-Cre hES cells

The ES cell line GENEA019 (GENEA Biocells; a female line) was used to generate hGPCs in this study. First, at the hES cell stage, Cre recombinase was inserted immediately downstream of the PDGFRA coding sequence and an intraribosomal entry site (IRES) to allow bicistronic expression. Donor DNA was generated by creating a plasmid containing 842 bp downstream and 875 bp upstream of the cut site for the homology arms. Between the homology arms and immediately after the stop codon, an IRES-Cre followed by both a puromycin selection gene under the PGK promoter and a polyadenylation sequence was added. The knock-in was performed by HDR-mediated insertion of the donor DNA, with the sgRNA targeting the PDGFRA gene using the following sequence, 5′-ctgtaactggcggattcgag-3′. This was targeted immediately after the stop of the coding sequence (Fig. 1a) and the sgRNA was inserted downstream of a U6 promoter in the pSpCas9(BB)-2A-Puro (PX459) plasmid (gift from F. Zhang; Addgene, 62988)34. This plasmid contains spCas9 expressed under a CBH promoter. Both the donor and the guide/Cas9 plasmid were electroporated into undifferentiated GENEA019 hES cells, as we previously described58. Briefly, hES cells were collected as a single-cell suspension following dissociation with Accutase (StemCell Technologies, 07920), washed in culture medium and counted with the automated cell counter NucleoCounter NC-200 (ChemoMetec). Per electroporation, a total of 1.5 × 106 cells were mixed with 5 µg of the AAVS1 targeting CRISPR–Cas9 plasmid (pXAT2) and 5 µg of reporter donor plasmid (pAAVS1-P-CAG-mCherry or pAAVS1-P-CAG-EGFP). pXAT2 (Addgene, plasmid 80494), pAAVS1-P-CAG-mCherry (Addgene, plasmid 80491) and pAAVS1-P-CAG-EGFP (Addgene, plasmid 80492) were gifts from K. Woltjen. Electroporation was performed using an Amaxa 4D-Nucleofector (Lonza) with the P3 primary cell kit (Lonza, V4XP-3024) according to the manufacturer’s guidelines. After nucleofection, the electroporated hES cell suspensions were transferred to 10-cm cell culture dishes and cultured with mTeSR1 supplemented with 10 µM Y-27632 (Tocris, 1254) for the first 24 h. Electroporated hES cells were grown for 48–72 h and then selected with puromycin (0.5 µg ml−1; Thermo Fisher, A1113803) until colonies were large enough to be picked up for expansion and genotyping. The genomic integrity of selected homozygous clones was assessed by karyotype and aCGH and a lack of major structural variants was confirmed before hGPC production, as described previously35.

Mouse models

For screening viruses in wild-type, nonchimeric mice, we purchased C57BL/6J mice from Jax Laboratories (Jackson Labs, 000664). For screening viruses in human glial chimeras, with an emphasis on targeting hGPCs and astrocytes, we used homozygous B6.129S7-Rag1tm1Mom/J (Rag1−/−) immunodeficient mice (Jackson Labs, 002216), chimerized neonatally with hES cell GPCs; the brains of these mice typically exhibited widespread colonization hGPCs and their derived astrocytes.

For screening potential oligodendrocyte-tropic viruses, we used Rag2-null immunodeficient and myelin-deficient mice chimerized neonatally with hES cell hGPCs. These mice reliably produced a largely humanized white matter. For this purpose, we bred homozygous C3HeB/FeJ MBPshi/shi shiverer mice (Jackson Laboratory, 001428) with homozygous C3H.129S6(B6)-Rag2tm1FwaN12 (rag2−/−) immunodeficient mice on the C3h background (Taconic, 000602-M) to produce myelin-deficient and immunodeficient mice, as described previously36.

All mice were housed in a temperature and humidity-controlled environment (64–73 °F, 30–70% humidity) within a pathogen-free colony room on a 12:12-h light cycle. They were given ad libitum access to Modified ProLab RMH 3000, 5P00 containing 0.025% trimethoprim and 0.124% sulfamethoxazole (Mod LabDiet 5P00) and autoclaved acid water (pH 2.5–3.0).

AAV production

AAV capsid library construction and amplification

To generate a viral library, we modified the M-CREATE strategy6,7. The plasmid to receive the 7-mer insert library was created using the backbone of the pAAV-MCS Expression Vector (Agilent, VPK-410). This backbone, including AAV2 ITRs, was fused to an expression cassette, in which a portion of the AAV2-P40-Rep2 was included for maintaining the expression of the capsid proteins and their proper splicing. The capsid sequence was modified by inserting a GCT codon (alanine) between Q574 and S575 of the AAV5 VP1 capsid to introduce an NheI cut site for insertion of a random Heptamer, as well as a silent mutation at L543 to destroy one of the XhoI sites and make it unique. The library was then created by amplifying the region between NheI and XhoI with AAV5-Cap-XhoI-Forward primer (5′-ccacgaccaataggatggagc-3′) and AAV5-Cap-NNN-NheI-Reverse primer (5′-gggcagtggtggagctagcNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNactagcctggttgttggtggc-3′). The PCR amplicon was then inserted into the capsid opened NheI and XhoI. Downstream of the Capsid gene, the construct also included two polyadenylation sequences, BGH (sense) and SV40 (anti-sense), both flanked by Lox66 and Lox71, to allow for a Cre-dependent single flip. The resulting library preps contained 4 × 105 variants by limiting dilution of the bacterial clones.

AAV2-Rep construct

To abolish the expression in cis of the native capsid and generate viral particles exclusively from the engineered capsid library, pAAV2/5 (Addgene, 104964; donated by M. Fan) was subjected to PCR-directed mutagenesis followed by recombination-mediated ligation (In-Fusion, Takara). In this construct (pAAV2/5-Rep2-ΔCap5-3STOP), the expression of Cap proteins was abrogated by introduction of nonsense mutations at the start of each of VP1 (Glu12Ter), VP2 (Lys6Ter) and VP3 (Gln14Ter). All cloning was performed using Takara Bio’s In-Fusion snap assembly kit (638952).

Generating capsids with evolved peptide inserts

After enriched capsid variants were recovered, their 21-bp sequences were ordered as primers and ligated into AAV5 Rep-Cap containing the NheI and XhoI cut sites to create pAAV2/5-574-Empty-Recipient plasmid. Individual 21-bp heptamer coding sequences for each viral variant (that is, CM1 or CM6) were then inserted by PCR at the NheI end and ligated to the vector open NheI/XhoI to create a separate rep-cap plasmid for each variant.

Viral amplification

For amplification of the final AAV library preps, HEK293FT cells were cultured in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 °C using 10% FBS formulated in high-glucose DMEM (Thermo Fisher). Previously published protocols59 were followed to produce the AAVs. Briefly, per viral prep, ten 150-mm plates of approximately 80% confluency HEK293FT cells were transfected with 0.4 mg of DNA-mix containing the Capsid, Helper (Addgene, 112867; a gift from J. Wilson) and AAV shuttle plasmids (pAAV-CAG-EGFP, pAAV-CAG-FLEX-EGFP and pAAV-CAG-FLEX-tdTomato; all gifts from E. Boyden; Addgene, 37825, 28306 and 28304, respectively) at a ratio of 0.6:0.3:0.1, along with PEI transfection reagent at a ratio of 1:4. The medium was replaced at 24 and 72 h after transfection and the cells and medium were harvested at 120 h. At both the 72-h and the 120-h time points, the medium was treated with polyethylene glycol for precipitating the virus. Cell pellets were lysed using salt active nuclease and the viral particles were separated by iodixanol density gradient solution spun using Type 60Ti rotor at 59,000 rpm for 3 h. The resulting viral band was further washed on an Amicon filter (30-kDa molecular weight cutoff; Millipore, UFC910024). The virus was then aliquoted and stored at −80 °C. Viral titers were established by way of a qPCR AAV titer kit (ABM, G931), as per the manufacturer’s instructions.

Production of hGPCs and establishment of human glial chimeric mice

GPCs were produced from PDGFRA-Cre transduced hES cells (GENEA019; Genea Biocells) or from unmodified hES cells (WA09 line, WiCell Research Institute) using our previously described protocol35. At 130–140 days in vitro, hGPCs were collected in Ca2+/Mg2+-free Hanks’ balanced salt solution (HBSS−/−; Thermo Fisher, 14170112), then mechanically dissociated to small clusters by gentle pipetting, spun and resuspended in cold HBSS−/− at 1 × 105 cells per µl. Newborn mice (postnatal day 1) received bilateral intrastriatal injections of 1 µl of cell suspension (that is, 1 × 105 hGPCs per hemisphere) under cryoanesthesia, as described previously35,36,37. The injection tracks permitted donor cell infiltration and chimerization of the overlying corpus callosum and cortex.

Cisternal viral delivery with systemic hypertonicity

At 12 weeks of age, the neonatally chimerized mice were anesthetized with ketamine (100 mg kg−1) and xylazine (10 mg kg−1) and monitored for anesthetic depth through cessation of toe pinch reflex. Mice were affixed in a stereotaxic frame and had their cisterna magna exposed. Surgical procedures were conducted as previously described30. A 30-gauge cannula, connected by PE10 tubing to a 100-µl Hamilton syringe (gas-tight 1700 series), was implanted into the cisterna magna and secured with cyanoacrylate glue and dental cement. Then, 10 µl of high-titer (>1012 genome copies per ml) AAV injectate was then infused into the cisterna magna at 2 µl min−1 using a syringe micropump (Harvard Apparatus). Concurrent with infusion start, the mice were administered 20 μl g−1 body weight IP injection of 1 M hypertonic saline as previously described27. Once the infusion was complete, mice were removed from the stereotaxic frame, placed either flat or in a prone Trendelenburg position on a −45° slope for 30 min and then returned to their home cage. Anesthetic depth was maintained for 1 h following the time at which the infusion began. Animals were given analgesic and allowed to recover for either 7 or 21 days, depending on the experiment, at which time the mice were anesthetized and killed by transcardiac perfusion with 20 ml of cold PBS followed by 20 ml 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA). Tissues were then isolated and immersion-fixed in 4% PFA for an additional 2 h.

AAV capsid identification

First, 1 week after injection, mice were killed and their livers, corpora callosa and striata were dissected. Genomic DNA was then extracted from either liver or pooled callosal and striatal cells using the Wizard SV Genomic DNA purification system (A2361). Next=generation sequencing libraries were generated using a nested PCR and PrimeSTAR GXL DNA polymerase (Takara Bio, R050A) to recover either recombined or nonrecombined viral genomes from the samples. The PCR consisted of ten cycles of outer PCR using the primers Cap-outer-Universal-Forward (5′-ctcatcaccagcgagagcg-3′) and either bGHPA-Reverse (5′-ccatagagcccaccgcatc-3′) or hGHPA-Recombined-Reverse (5′-cacaaataaagcactctggtatgacaag-3′) for nonrecombined and recombined sequences, respectively. After that, forward (5′-TCGTCGGCAGCGTCAGATGTGTATAAGAGACAGNNNNNNNNcgtacaacgtcggcgggcagat-3′) and reverse (5′-GTCTCGTGGGCTCGGAGATGTGTATAAGAGACAGNNNNNNNNggcacaggcgtgttcttgatgag-3′) primers with Illumina adaptors and sample indices added were spiked into the reactions and run for 25 more cycles to give each sample a unique barcode. These products were run on a 1% agarose gel and the expected 352-bp band was purified. Final samples were indexed using NexteraXT Indexing and sequencing on an Illumina MiSeq V2 Nano (150-bp paired-end reads).

Following sequencing and demultiplexing, all analysis was carried out in R version 4.2.3; the 21-bp DNA sequences, corresponding to the random heptamer coding sequences, were extracted on the basis of their flanking sequences. Each sample was then normalized by sequencing depth and converted into 7-aa peptide sequences using Biostrings version 2.66.0. Within individual animal libraries, recombined brain libraries were then evaluated for enrichment versus nonrecombined brain or liver libraries. From each mouse, the top five most enriched human-selective heptamers were used to generated viral particles for downstream testing. For the generation of Venn diagram intersections, capsid sequences with a greater than 98% organ specificity (defined as the percentage of an organ’s reads among all reads) were considered selective for hGPCs, mouse brain or mouse liver.

All code for our analysis of capsid identification and assignment may be found on GitHub (https://github.com/CTNGoldmanLab/hGPC_AAV_Capsid_Evolution).

Tissue processing and immunohistochemistry

Mice were transcardially perfused with HBSS (Thermo Fisher Scientific) followed by 4% PFA in 0.1 M PBS at 3 weeks following AAV delivery. The brains, livers, spleens and kidneys were dissected, postfixed in 4% PFA for 2 h and stored in 1× PBS. Tissues for histology were cryopreserved in 0.1 M PBS + 30% sucrose before embedding in optimal cutting temperature (OCT) compound. Cryopreserved organs were sagittally sectioned into 20-μm-thick serial sections using a cryostat.

To quantify plasma viral concentrations, mice were first anesthetized and then given either an intracisternal or intravenous injection of 10 µl of virus at 1 × 1012 vg per ml. Then, 10 min later, the mice were exsanguinated through inferior vena cava blood draw, their blood was spun into heparinized tubes and their plasma was taken for PCR quantification. To quantify peripheral viral concentrations, the liver, kidney, spleen and dorsal root ganglia were collected 24 h after intracisternal or intravenous viral injection, after perfusion with 4% PFA.

Brain sections were blocked with 5% normal goat serum in 0.1 M PBS + 0.3% Triton-X for 1 h at room temperature and incubated overnight at 4 °C with primary antibodies against EGFP (chicken, 1:1,000; Thermo Fisher Scientific), human nuclear antigen (hNA; mouse, 1:400; Abcam ab254080), PDGFRA (rabbit, 1:600; Cell Signaling Technology, 5231S), Olig2 (rabbit, 1:500; Millipore), NeuN (rabbit, 1:800; Millipore), Sox9 (rabbit, 1:500; Abcam), myelin basic protein (MBP; 1:400; Abcam, ab40390), anti-dtTomato (1:400; Invitrogen, M11217), cyclic nucleotide diphosphatase (CNPase; 1:800; Abcam, ab18527), NeuN (1:400; Millipore, ABN78MI) or HA tag (mouse, 1:400; Invitrogen). Immunostaining was visualized with species-specific secondary antibodies conjugated with AlexaFluor-488, AlexaFluor-568 (1:400; Invitrogen) or AlexaFluor-647 (1:200; Invitrogen). Lastly, sections were coverslipped with mounting medium containing DAPI (Vector Laboratories). Slides were sealed and imaged with Olympus Slideview vs200 or a Leica DM6000B confocal microscope, and then analyzed using Stereo Investigator version 2019.

qPCR

For reverse transcription PCR, tissues from spleen, kidney, liver and thoracic dorsal root ganglia were homogenized and DNA extracted using the Wizard genomic DNA purification kit (Promega) following the manufacturer’s protocol. qPCR was run using TaqMan probes for the 18S ribosomal gene and EGFP (Thermo Fisher, Mr04329676 and Mm03928990, respectively). For digital PCR, used for assessment of viral genomes in plasma, spun mouse plasma was incubated for 3 min at 25 °C using viral lysis buffer from the qPCR AAV titer kit to release the viral genomes (Applied Biological Materials, G931). Viral genome quantification was then performed using the Thermo Fisher QuantStudio AbsoluteQ dPCR instrument with a TaqMan probe for EGFP (Thermo Fisher, Mm03928990). All PCR data were analyzed in R as one-way ANOVAs with Tukey post hoc tests and visualized using ggplot2.

Primary hepatocyte culture and AAV transduction

Human and mouse primary hepatocytes were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (human hepatocytes, PCS-450-010; mouse hepatocytes, AML12). Cells were maintained in HepatoXcell primary hepatocyte maintenance medium according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Hepatocytes were seeded at a density of 6.0 × 105 cells per well in poly(ornithine)-coated (Sigma-Aldrich, P4957-50ML) six-well tissue-culture-treated plates (Fisher Scientific, 07-200-83). Cells were transduced with AAV-CM1 or AAV5 in complete medium at a final concentration of 2.5 × 105 viral particles per ml, corresponding to the viral genome copy number detected in mouse plasma following cisterna magna injection. Cells were maintained for 5 days after transduction and harvested using TrypLE Select enzyme (Invitrogen, 12563-011); genomic DNA was isolated using the Wizard genomic DNA purification kit (Promega, PR-A2361).

Fluorescence in situ hybridization

Mice (14 weeks-old) engrafted neonatally at PND1 with G19-derived PDGFRA-Cre GPCs were anesthetized and rapidly decapitated. Brains were then isolated, frozen in chilled isopentane, embedded in OCT (TissueTek), sectioned sagittally at 10 μm on a cryostat and stored at −80 °C. Slides with tissue sections were immersed in 4% PFA for 15 min, rinsed twice with 1× PBS and dehydrated at room temperature in increasing ethanol solutions: 50%, 70%, 100% and 100% for 5 min each. Using the RNAscope multiplex fluorescent reagent kit (323100, ACDBio), tissue sections were incubated in hydrogen peroxide for 10 min in a humidified box, rinsed with distilled water and incubated with protease III for 30 min at room temperature. Hybridization probes against Cre recombinase (312281-C1, ACDBio), human PDGFRa (604481-C2, ACDBio) and human Olig2 (424191-C3, ACDBio) or human NeuN (415591-C3, ACDBio) were then incubated for 2 h at 40°C. Control probes were also incubated in separate tissue sections. Probe signals were then serially amplified for 30 min at 40 °C each and individually developed starting with Opal 520 (1:750; FP1487001KT, PerkinElmer), followed by Opal 570 (1:750; FP1488001KT, PerkinElmer) and then Opal 690 (1:1500; FP1497001KT, PerkinElmer). Slides were coverslipped with Vectashield with DAPI for imaging.

Statistics and reproducibility

All experiments were repeated as indicated by their sample sizes. Immunostainings were repeated in 3–5 separate animals. All statistical analyses were performed in R, as noted.

articles.title: Verification of the Outer Space Treaty with cosmic protons
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 08 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10783-2 By observing the neutrons induced by spallation from protons in the inner Van Allen radiation belts using widely available sensor technologies, the detection of a thermonuclear weapon carried by a space sat...
articles.content:
Abstract

The Outer Space Treaty (OST) was opened to signatures in 1967 and, since then, 117 countries, including China, the USA and Russia, have become part of it1. Among other stipulations, the treaty bans the placement of nuclear weapons in outer space. Recently, the US government has raised worries that Russia is testing nuclear-armed anti-satellite weapon (ASAT) components, with the possibility that it will place a nuclear weapon in space. Such a device, if detonated, would destroy most of the satellites in the low Earth orbit. This danger is compounded by the lack of a verification mechanism for the OST. No methodologies of verification have been proposed in the open peer-reviewed literature. Here a concept and feasibility study is presented for verifying a satellite’s compliance to the OST by observing the neutrons induced by spallation from the approximately GeV protons in the inner Van Allen radiation belts2. The calculations show that a 9U-CubeSat-sized detection platform can identify a thermonuclear weapon from a distance of 4 km in approximately one week of observation. This conceptual study will stimulate and inform future research and development of verification platforms for the OST.

articles.title: LARES-2 satellite measures frame-dragging effect around the Earth
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 08 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10715-0 Using LARES-2, LAGEOS and GRACE satellites, researchers measured Earth’s frame-dragging with unprecedented precision, strongly confirming general relativity, constraining alternative gravity theories, and i...
articles.content:
Abstract

Laser-ranging provides some of the most precise tests of gravity in the weak-field regime, enabling experimental probes of Einstein’s general theory of relativity using the Earth as a laboratory1. A central test of general relativity is the amplitude of frame-dragging, that is, the dragging of spacetime by a rotating mass2,3,4,5. Owing to its optimized orbit, a very low surface-to-mass ratio and a highly uniform retroreflector distribution, we show that the recently launched Laser Relativity Satellite 2 (LARES-2)6—together with its predecessor LAGEOS and the GRACE satellites—enables a measurement of terrestrial frame-dragging with a relative uncertainty at the one-part-in-a-thousand level, representing an order-of-magnitude improvement over previous Solar System determinations. This result provides a stringent confirmation of general relativity in the near-Earth environment and places strong constraints on alternative gravitational models that predict deviations specifically in frame-dragging, including scalar–tensor extensions such as Chern–Simons gravity7,8. Beyond tests of fundamental physics, the combined analysis of LARES-2 and LAGEOS also improves the determination of Earth’s lunisolar tides, illustrating the broader geophysical impact of high-precision relativistic satellite experiments.

articles.title: James Webb Space Telescope celebrates its 4th birthday with stunning image of a galaxy crash site
articles.summary: To celebrate the fourth birthday of the James Webb Space Telescope, NASA has released a stunning image of the strangely shaped galaxy Centaurus A, the site of a cosmic collision 2 billion years ago.
articles.content:
July 2026 marks four years since NASA's James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) images were first revealed to the general public, marking a new era for astronomy. To celebrate this anniversary of the most powerful space telescope ever launched, NASA has released a stunning image of the strangely shaped galaxy called Centaurus A.
Located around 11 million light-years away, Centaurus A owes its unusual structure to a collision between two galaxies around 2 billion years ago. This merger provided the galaxy with an abundance of gas and dust, the raw material for intense star formation. It also supplied the supermassive black hole at the heart of this galaxy with an abundant supply of the same matter to feed upon and power a bright and violent central region, or active galactic nucleus (AGN), as that central area blasts out powerful, high-speed jets of plasma.
Though this galaxy is much closer to us than many of the early galaxies that the JWST has studied in its four years of operations, that doesn't mean it's any less useful. In fact, with its ultra-sensitive infrared vision, the JWST has been able to peer into the heart and inner workings of Centaurus A like no telescope that has gone before.

"No single telescope tells the whole story," Shawn Domagal-Goldman, division director of Astrophysics at NASA Headquarters in Washington, said in a statement. "Discoveries build over time, and new observatories expand on the foundations laid by earlier missions. The JWST represents the most powerful step forward yet, opening a window into wavelengths and details never before accessible.
"This allows astronomers to examine structures and processes that other telescopes could not see."

Building upon the legacy of Spitzer and Hubble

The key to the new view of Centaurus A that the JWST has been able to provide is the space telescope's powerful infrared vision. The thick dust that crowds the heart of this galaxy blocks visible light the Hubble Space Telescope previously relied on to study it. Infrared light is able to slip through these dense sheets of gas and dust.
The now-retired Spitzer Space Telescope had previously studied Centaurus A in infrared, but while it could resolve larger structures in the galaxy, it lacked the observational power to resolve individual stars and finer details.

However, even though the JWST has been able to use its MIRI (Mid-Infrared Instrument) and NIRCam (Near-Infrared Camera) to study Centaurus A like never before, there are still mysteries to be solved about this structure.
For example, in the MIRI image of Centaurus A, alongside the glowing stellar nurseries where new stars are born and spit gas and dust into their surroundings, there is a curious S-shaped feature. Scientists still don't know how this structure was formed and if the active black hole at the heart of Centaurus A played a role in its creation.
The JWST images of Centaurus A do reveal plenty about the role this galaxy's central black hole plays in carving out its morphology. For example, the JWST was able to see fast-moving ionized gas being shunted outwards by the black hole’s activity. The JWST data also revealed warmer molecular hydrogen in a warped and rotating disk near the heart of Centaurus A.

This data seems to show how a galaxy's central black hole can trigger bouts of intense star formation by condensing gas and dust, but also how these cosmic titans can stunt star birth and "kill" their host galaxies by purging the raw material needed for the star-formation process.

That means that thanks to the JWST, scientists are now building a more comprehensive cosmic history of Centaurus A, promising discoveries that can be applied to other galaxies to build a better picture of how the universe has evolved.

Here's to another four years of cosmic discoveries.

articles.title: Our Milky Way galaxy might be larger than we thought
articles.summary: Astronomers used cosmic explosions to find that we may be wrong about our own galaxy.
articles.content:
Is the Milky Way even bigger than we thought? New observations have revealed that our galaxy's spiral arms could stretch farther and wider than we previously concluded.

The Milky Way's spiral structure was discovered over 175 years ago in 1850. But new information could completely change our understanding of our cosmic home. Astronomers have taken a new look at our Milky Way galaxy using data from NASA's Chandra X-ray observatory and the European Space Agency's XMM-Newton observatory and have pieced together new, precise measurements of the galaxy's spiral arms. And what they found is that its spiral arms stretch out farther than we once thought, a discovery that could change our understanding of our galaxy's structure.

"The differences are small, but any revision of these distances is important because they are so fundamental for understanding our galaxy," co-author Ilaria Fornasiero said in a statement. "For example, this could mean that astronomers have to revise estimates of the mass of the galaxy, because that affects how wide the arms stretch."

To make this new galactic measurement, researchers had to get a little creative with the data. They measured these cosmic distances by observing X-ray light scattered by the dust in the Milky Way's arms as it echoed out from around gamma-ray bursts, or the most powerful explosions across the universe that happen either when massive stars collapse or neutron stars collide and merge. These massive bursts of energy are happening far beyond our galaxy, but their X-ray light is so powerful that it can reach and bounce off of dust clouds in the Milky Way's arms.

By studying the diameters of the rings of light as they expand away from these explosions and observing how and where they reflect off of the Milky Way's dust, the team was able to precisely point to where the galaxy's arms extend.

"This is a very direct way – relying only on geometry – to precisely measure distances to the Milky Way's spiral arms," lead author Beatrice Vaia, who led this research as a PhD student, said in the statement. "Most other methods rely on assumptions about how the Milky Way rotates, which become increasingly uncertain in the outer regions of our galaxy."

The team used the X-ray light from three different gamma-ray bursts to look at three of the Milky Way's spiral arms: the Perseus, the Outer, and the Outer-Scutum-Centaurus arms. According to these new measurements, both the Outer and the Outer Scutum-Centaurus arms are about ten percent more distant than was previously thought.

With this data, the team was also able to measure the thickness of the Milky Way's most distant arm, which they found to be about 3,500 light-years wide. By incorporating the arm's width, the team ensured that they were measuring the full extension of the arm and not just one particular dust cloud, further bolstering their findings.

While it's interesting that the Milky Way's arms extend out a bit farther and wider than we previously thought, these new findings could have larger implications. Based on these new measurements, astronomers may have to reinvestigate our understanding of our galaxy's mass distribution, rotation and overall structure. This evolving understanding could ripple out and impact how we view not just the structure but the evolution of our galaxy and beyond.

But this study isn't one that can be replicated too easily. That's because gamma-ray bursts don't happen all of the time. Even more rare are bursts that we can see clearly through our galaxy.

"We’re relying on the universe to provide us with these events, and so far, over 25 years, we’ve only found a handful that we can use," co-author Andrea Tiengo of Scuola Universitaria Superiore IUSS Pavia said in the same statement. "That said, we will continue to be on the lookout for more."

This work was described in a new study published June 19 in the journal Astronomy & Astrophysics.

articles.title: 'Project Hail Mary' is finally streaming Amazon Prime Video, and we can't wait to watch it again
articles.summary: 'Project Hail Mary' blasted off onto Prime Video starting on July 3.
articles.content:
Amazon MGM Studios' "Project Hail Mary," the science fiction blockbuster starring Ryan Gosling that launched into theaters back on March 20, 2026 is now hitting its streaming stride on Prime Video after a stop on MGM+.

The $200 million outer space saga, directed by Phil Lord and Christopher Miller ("The Lego Movie," "Spider-Man: Into the Spider-Verse") and adapted from author Andy Weir's 2021 novel of the same name, made a respectable $630 million in its full theatrical run.

Despite a general jokiness and long runtime, the film was well-received by critics and audiences and now those who've held out from going to the multiplex to absorb the adventure will have a chance to catch it at home.

Gosling portrays Ryland Grace, a middle-grade teacher and molecular biologist who reluctantly gets recruited for a desperate deep space mission to try and solve a cosmic conundrum that's causing our sun to slowly dim.

Along the way to the Tau Ceti star cluster, Grace encounters a rock-skinned alien whose homeworld suffers from the same problem and together they hatch a plan to rescue their prospective planets from nasty sun-munching microbes called astrophages.

"Ryan added so much depth and layers to Ryland that I never had in the book," Weir told Space in a March interview. "And I was so happy about that because I consider character depth to be one of my biggest weaknesses as an author. I'm a plot-driven author. Seeing Ryan add all these layers, I'm like, 'Oh, good, he's covering the things that I didn’t do.' Then later I'll get credit for that character.”

"Project Hail Mary" has had a circuitous route onto home video in the aftermath of its big screen campaign, first rolling out on video-on-demand May 12, then leaping onto Amazon-owned MGM+ on June 18 for its streaming debut to help bolster subscriber numbers for that smaller platform.

And finally, the popular sci-fi epic is settling in for what's hopefully a long stay on Prime Video that started on July 3, just in time for the long Independence Day weekend in the United States. Amaze!
